question: A `@JobWorker(type = "rest")` makes a blocking `RestTemplate` call and the team needs more throughput without rewriting in reactive style. Which single change does the docs suggest?

options.text: Build the client with `.numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5)` (or the equivalent Spring Boot Starter configuration) so the polling component dispatches jobs across a thread pool.
Bump `retries` on the service task definition above the default 3 so blocking jobs are reprocessed faster.
Set `autoComplete = true` on the `@JobWorker`, which switches the SDK to a non-blocking completion path.
Lower the broker's job activation timeout so blocking jobs are reassigned to other workers sooner.

a.text: The docs show exactly this fix for blocking handlers: build the `ZeebeClient` with `.numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5)` (or the equivalent Spring Boot Starter execution-threads configuration). This lifts the default-of-one thread cap so blocking REST calls can run in parallel.

b.text: Retries control failure handling, not concurrency. Raising retries does not make a single worker process more jobs per second.

c.text: `autoComplete` controls whether the SDK auto-sends the complete command; it does not change the threading model. Blocking code still serializes on the same execution threads.

d.text: Shortening the activation timeout causes jobs to be re-activated by other workers and increases broker load; it does not raise throughput on the original worker.

explanation: The docs' direct fix for scaling blocking Java workers without rewriting in reactive style is to enlarge the worker execution thread pool — for example with `ZeebeClient.newClientBuilder().numJobWorkerExecutionThreads(5).build()` or the equivalent Spring Boot Starter configuration. Throughput then scales roughly with the number of threads, bounded by the I/O latency.
